skill.name: plugin-structure
skill.description: Guides Claude Code and Codex users through plugin directory layouts, manifest configuration, component organization, hooks, MCP servers, and portable path conventions.
skill.summary: Build well-structured Claude Code plugins with correct manifests, components, examples, and path conventions.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, filesystem, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, including a critical combination heuristic. Manual review found these are documentation examples and plugin scaffolding guidance rather than active executable code, with no prompt injection markers found. The skill still deserves a medium warning because it teaches MCP server command configuration, hook scripts, and environment variable propagation that users must review before running.

medium_findings.title: Command-Running Plugin Patterns Require Review
medium_findings.description: The skill documents MCP server command fields and shell hook scripts. This is legitimate plugin development guidance, but generated plugins may execute local commands if users adopt these examples without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly show MCP command configuration and bash hook execution patterns. The package is documentation rather than active code, so the risk is indirect and depends on generated plugin usage.

medium_findings.title: Credential Environment Variables in MCP Examples
medium_findings.description: Examples pass API keys and GitHub tokens into MCP server definitions. This is a normal configuration pattern, but it can expose secrets to plugin servers if a generated plugin is untrusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly include secret-bearing environment variables, but no evidence shows exfiltration or malicious endpoints. Users should review generated MCP servers before granting tokens.

low_findings.title: Static Command Execution Hits Are Mostly Markdown Fence Matches
low_findings.description: The analyzer flagged many Ruby backtick and shell patterns, but reviewed locations are Markdown fences, directory trees, usage transcripts, or illustrative snippets. They are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited evidence is plain Markdown documentation and sample terminal output. There is no runtime wrapper, interpreter call, or user-controlled command injection path in these locations.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Pattern Is Shown as a Prohibited Example
low_findings.description: The path traversal sequence appears in a rules section that tells users parent directory navigation is not allowed. This is defensive documentation, not a traversal implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The same section explicitly states that parent directory navigation is forbidden. The dangerous string is presented as an invalid example.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholder Metadata Examples
low_findings.description: The detected URLs are example homepage and repository metadata for sample plugins. No evidence shows runtime network access or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear inside manifest metadata examples, not request code. They do not receive secrets or trigger network calls in the reviewed content.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Pattern Is Naming and Text Context
low_findings.description: Several weak-crypto alerts appear to be caused by words inside descriptions, names, examples, or documentation text rather than cryptographic operations. No hashing or encryption implementation was confirmed in reviewed snippets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are frontmatter, Markdown command examples, and directory naming examples. I did not find evidence of actual weak cryptographic algorithm use in these reviewed locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: MCP Command Configuration Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows plugin manifests that start MCP servers through command and args fields. Treat generated MCP server configurations as executable integrations that require user review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly configure commands that launch local server processes. The pattern is legitimate for plugins but remains security-sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hook Script Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The examples include shell hook scripts that inspect git state and run linters. Users should inspect generated hooks before enabling them in a repository.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippets are explicit bash examples that run local commands. They are instructional examples, so the risk is not malicious by itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Critical Combination Heuristic Dismissed After Context Review
dangerous_patterns.description: The scanner combined command, network, filesystem, and credential indicators into a critical heuristic. Manual review found these indicators in documentation examples, not a coordinated malicious workflow.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package intent is plugin architecture documentation and examples. I found no evidence of prompt injection, hidden execution, credential exfiltration, or malicious endpoint usage.

content.value_statement: Plugin authors need a reliable structure for manifests, commands, agents, skills, hooks, and MCP servers. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through consistent plugin scaffolding and review-ready organization.
actual_capabilities: Explains the required plugin directory layout and manifest location.
Guides creation of commands, agents, skills, hooks, scripts, and MCP server folders.
Documents plugin.json metadata, component paths, and auto-discovery behavior.
Shows how to use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for portable plugin paths.
Provides minimal, standard, and advanced plugin examples for different complexity levels.
Lists naming, portability, organization, and troubleshooting practices for plugin authors.
limitations: It provides documentation and scaffolding guidance, not a packaged plugin generator executable.
Generated hooks, scripts, and MCP servers still need manual security review before use.
It focuses on Claude Code plugin architecture and may not apply to unrelated plugin systems.
It does not validate marketplace policy, package provenance, or third-party server behavior.

use_cases.title: Create a First Plugin
use_cases.description: Use the minimal example to create a single-command plugin with the required manifest and directory structure.

use_cases.title: Organize Production Components
use_cases.description: Use the standard structure to group commands, agents, skills, hooks, and references in a maintainable layout.

use_cases.title: Review Enterprise Plugin Architecture
use_cases.description: Use the advanced patterns to reason about MCP servers, shared libraries, configuration, monitoring, and portability.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Minimal Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a minimal Claude Code plugin named my-helper with one command. Include the required directory layout and explain each file.

prompt_templates.title: Add Core Components
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Claude Code plugin with commands, one agent, one skill, and hooks. Use standard directories and portable path references.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Manifest
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this plugin.json for structure, metadata, component paths, and portability issues. Explain required corrections before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Advanced Plugin
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan an advanced Claude Code plugin with MCP servers, shared scripts, configuration, and monitoring. Include security review points for executable components.

output: A concise directory plan with a manifest folder and one command file.
A short explanation of how auto-discovery loads the command.
A checklist for naming, metadata, and installation review.
A list of valid and invalid component paths.
Corrections for relative paths, custom component folders, and portability.
Warnings for hooks, MCP servers, and environment variables that need manual review.

output_examples.input: Plan a plugin with hooks and MCP servers.
output: A component map covering commands, agents, skills, hooks, scripts, and MCP server configuration.
Security notes for command execution, credentials, and generated server code.
Maintenance guidance for versioning, documentation, and testing.

best_practices: Start with the minimal structure, then add components only when they serve a clear purpose.
Use ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} for internal paths so plugins stay portable across systems.
Review every generated hook, script, and MCP server before granting filesystem, network, or token access.
anti_patterns: Do not place commands, agents, skills, or hooks inside the manifest directory.
Do not use absolute paths or parent directory traversal in plugin component paths.
Do not pass secrets to generated MCP servers unless the server code and endpoint behavior are trusted.

faq.question: What does this skill help create?
faq.answer: It helps design Claude Code plugin structures, manifests, component folders, examples, and review checklists.

faq.question: Does it generate executable plugins by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples that an AI assistant can use when creating or reviewing plugin files.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Why is the risk level medium?
faq.answer: The skill documents hooks, scripts, MCP command execution, and secret environment variables. These patterns require review before use.

faq.question: Does it contain prompt injection text?
faq.answer: No prompt injection markers were found during this review.

faq.question: What should users review before publishing a generated plugin?
faq.answer: Users should review manifest paths, executable scripts, hooks, MCP servers, network endpoints, and environment variable handling.
